questions.question: Is the SMP aligned with the risk profile of the mAb/bispecific, including biologic-specific risks (immunogenicity, infusion reactions, CRS, immune-mediated AEs, target-mediated toxicity)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether the SMP incorporates biologic-specific risk categories: immunogenicity risk (ADA impact on PK, efficacy, and safety); infusion/injection reaction risk and management; cytokine release syndrome risk for bispecific T-cell engagers; immune-mediated adverse events (autoimmune-like events for immunomodulatory biologics); target-mediated toxicity from on-target/off-tumour effects; Fc-mediated effector function toxicity (ADCC, CDC); and risk stratification by treatment phase (first dose vs maintenance).
questions.rationale: Biologic safety profiles are fundamentally different from small molecules. The SMP must address risks that have no small molecule parallel: immunogenicity, CRS, immune-mediated AEs, and target biology-driven toxicity. For bispecifics, the SMP must address dual-target biology risks including on-target/off-tumour effects from co-expression patterns. A generic SMP that does not explicitly address these biologic-specific risks is inadequate for regulatory review and internal governance.

questions.question: Are roles and responsibilities for safety surveillance clearly defined, including biologic-specific safety roles (immunogenicity safety lead, CRS management medical monitor, infusion reaction adjudicator)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether safety governance includes biologic-specific roles: designated immunogenicity safety lead responsible for ADA impact assessment; CRS management medical monitor for bispecific programs; infusion/injection reaction adjudicator; immune-mediated AE specialist reviewer; and clear interfaces between clinical safety, bioanalytical/immunogenicity teams, and clinical pharmacology for integrated safety-PK-immunogenicity assessment.
questions.rationale: Biologic safety surveillance requires specialized expertise that does not exist in small molecule programs. Immunogenicity data interpretation requires understanding of assay limitations and PK relationships. CRS grading and management requires oncology/immunology expertise. These biologic-specific safety competencies must be explicitly assigned, not assumed to be covered by general medical monitoring.

questions.question: Is there a functioning Safety Review Committee (SRC) and/or Data Monitoring Committee (DMC) with biologic-specific competency (immunology, immunogenicity interpretation, CRS management)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether safety governance bodies include biologic-specific expertise: DMC members with immunology/biologic safety experience; SRC with competency in immunogenicity data interpretation; CRS management expertise for bispecific programs; external advisory capability for novel biologic safety signals; and defined triggers for ad hoc safety reviews based on biologic-specific thresholds (ADA incidence, infusion reaction rate, immune-mediated AE clustering).
questions.rationale: Safety committees for biologic programs must include expertise in biologic-specific safety assessment. A DMC composed only of clinicians and statisticians without immunology expertise cannot adequately interpret immunogenicity signals, CRS patterns, or immune-mediated adverse events. Biologic-specific competency should be a selection criterion for DMC/SRC membership.

questions.question: Are AEs, SAEs, and AESIs systematically collected and classified, with biologic-specific AESIs pre-defined (infusion reactions, injection-site reactions, CRS, immune-mediated AEs, immunogenicity-related events, opportunistic infections)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether AESI definitions include biologic-specific categories: infusion-related reactions (graded per NCI-CTCAE or protocol-specific scale); injection-site reactions for SC products; cytokine release syndrome (graded per Lee criteria or ASTCT consensus); immune-mediated adverse events (autoimmune-like events categorized by organ system); immunogenicity-related clinical events (hypersensitivity, anaphylaxis, loss of efficacy potentially related to ADA); opportunistic infections for immunosuppressive biologics; and target-mediated on-target toxicities specific to the biologic mechanism.
questions.rationale: Biologic programs require pre-defined AESIs that reflect the known class risks of mAb/bispecific therapy. Without pre-defined biologic-specific AESIs, safety signals are buried in generic MedDRA coding and detected late. Regulators expect sponsors to prospectively define and monitor biologic-specific AESIs. The AESI list should be mechanism-informed, class-informed, and phase-appropriate.

questions.question: Are AE collection methods standardized across sites, with specific guidance for biologic-specific AE capture (infusion reaction documentation, CRS grading, injection-site assessment, temporal relationship to dosing)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether AE collection standardization addresses: infusion reaction documentation (onset time, symptoms, severity grade, interventions, outcome, relationship to infusion rate); CRS grading using standardized criteria (Lee/ASTCT); injection-site reaction assessment (size, duration, photography if applicable); temporal relationship to dosing (time from infusion/injection to AE onset); relationship to ADA status (AEs in context of immunogenicity data); and standardized collection of immune-mediated AE workup data (autoantibodies, organ-specific labs).
questions.rationale: Inconsistent AE collection across sites obscures biologic-specific safety signals. Infusion reactions must be captured with standardized timing and grading. CRS grading varies dramatically between sites without standardized criteria. Injection-site reactions require consistent assessment methodology. Temporal relationship to dosing is critical for distinguishing biologic-specific AEs from background events.

questions.question: Are electronic data capture (EDC) and medical coding systems validated and configured for biologic-specific safety data capture (infusion reaction eCRF modules, CRS grading forms, immunogenicity-linked AE flagging)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether EDC systems include biologic-specific modules: dedicated infusion reaction capture pages with standardized fields; CRS grading eCRF per ASTCT/Lee criteria; injection-site reaction assessment forms; automated flagging of AEs potentially related to immunogenicity (linking AE data with ADA status); temporal relationship fields linking AEs to dosing events; and MedDRA coding conventions for biologic-specific events (preferred terms, SMQs for infusion reactions, anaphylaxis, CRS).
questions.rationale: EDC systems configured for small molecule trials lack the data capture fields needed for biologic-specific safety events. Infusion reactions, CRS, and injection-site reactions require dedicated eCRF modules with standardized grading fields. Automated linkage between AE data and immunogenicity status enables real-time safety signal detection. Generic AE capture forms miss critical biologic-specific data elements.

questions.question: Are SAEs being reported within required timelines, with expedited processes for biologic-specific serious events (anaphylaxis, severe CRS, serious infusion reactions, serious immune-mediated events)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether SAE reporting includes expedited pathways for biologic-specific serious events: anaphylaxis requiring immediate 24-hour expedited reporting; severe CRS (Grade 3+) with rapid medical review and reporting; serious infusion reactions leading to hospitalization; serious immune-mediated adverse events (hepatitis, pneumonitis, nephritis, colitis); and immunogenicity-related serious events (serum sickness-like reactions, severe hypersensitivity).
questions.rationale: Certain biologic-specific SAEs require more rapid reporting and medical review than standard timelines accommodate. Anaphylaxis and severe CRS are acute, life-threatening events that may signal a safety concern requiring immediate action. Delays in reporting these events can affect other patients on treatment and may indicate a need for protocol modification. Expedited processes for biologic-specific SAEs must be defined in the SMP.

questions.question: Are SAEs reconciled between clinical and safety databases?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether SAE reconciliation between clinical database, safety database, and regulatory submissions is performed on schedule and documented.
questions.rationale: SAE reconciliation is a fundamental pharmacovigilance requirement. This applies equally to biologic and small molecule programs.

questions.question: Is there clear medical review and causality assessment for all SAEs, with biologic-specific causality considerations (temporal relationship to infusion, ADA status at time of event, mechanism-based plausibility)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether causality assessment incorporates biologic-specific considerations: temporal relationship to infusion/injection (onset during or shortly after dosing suggests infusion reaction/CRS); ADA status at time of event (ADA-positive patients may have altered PK and different AE profile); mechanism-based plausibility (target biology-driven on-target toxicity vs off-target); class effect assessment (known risks of mAb class or mechanism); and distinction between drug-related and disease-related events in context of immunomodulatory biologics.
questions.rationale: Causality assessment for biologic SAEs requires biologic-specific reasoning that goes beyond standard small molecule criteria. The temporal relationship to infusion is a critical signal for infusion reactions and CRS. ADA status at the time of the event provides context for PK-related AEs. Mechanism-based plausibility requires understanding of target biology. Generic causality frameworks miss these biologic-specific dimensions.

questions.question: Is there a global process for unblinding in life-threatening cases, with specific provisions for biologic-specific emergencies (anaphylaxis, severe CRS) requiring immediate treatment decisions?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether emergency unblinding processes account for biologic-specific acute emergencies: anaphylaxis requiring immediate epinephrine and knowledge of treatment assignment; severe CRS requiring tocilizumab or corticosteroid intervention; severe infusion reactions requiring treatment-specific management; and immune-mediated organ toxicity requiring urgent immunosuppression.
questions.rationale: Biologic-specific emergencies (anaphylaxis, severe CRS) may require immediate knowledge of treatment assignment for appropriate clinical management. Standard unblinding timelines may be too slow for these acute events. Emergency unblinding protocols must account for the speed required in biologic-specific life-threatening situations.

questions.question: Are statistical methods and thresholds for signal detection defined, including biologic-specific signal detection approaches (immunogenicity-safety correlation, exposure-safety analysis, temporal pattern analysis relative to dosing cycles)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether signal detection methodology includes biologic-specific approaches: correlation analysis between ADA status and AE incidence/severity; exposure-safety relationship analysis (drug level at time of AE); temporal pattern analysis relative to infusion/dosing cycles (e.g., first-dose vs later cycles); CRS incidence monitoring with cycle-specific thresholds; infusion reaction rate monitoring with trend detection; and dose-response analysis for safety signals across dose cohorts.
questions.rationale: Biologic safety signals often have unique temporal and exposure-dependent patterns. CRS typically occurs with early dosing cycles and diminishes over time. Infusion reactions may increase with repeated dosing due to immunogenicity. ADA-related safety events correlate with immunogenicity status. Standard signal detection methods that ignore these biologic-specific patterns will miss or misinterpret safety signals.

questions.question: Is signal detection based on aggregate data review (e.g., by SMQs, PTs, SOCs), including biologic-specific SMQs (anaphylactic/anaphylactoid reactions, infusion-related reactions, immune-mediated disorders)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether aggregate safety review uses biologic-relevant MedDRA SMQs: anaphylactic/anaphylactoid reactions (broad and narrow); infusion-related reactions; hypersensitivity reactions; immune-mediated/autoimmune disorders; cytokine release syndrome; injection-site reactions; and opportunistic infections (for immunosuppressive biologics). Also evaluates use of customized biologic-specific SMQs beyond standard MedDRA.
questions.rationale: Standard MedDRA SMQs may not adequately capture all biologic-specific safety signals. Custom SMQs for CRS, immunogenicity-related events, and mechanism-specific toxicities may be needed. Signal detection using biologic-relevant SMQs enables earlier detection of biologic-specific safety concerns.

questions.question: Are data reviewed for temporal trends, dose dependence, and organ system clustering, including biologic-specific patterns (cycle dependence, immunogenicity-temporal correlation, first-dose effects vs late-onset events)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether safety data review includes biologic-specific pattern analysis: cycle-dependent patterns (CRS decreasing over cycles, infusion reactions increasing with immunogenicity); immunogenicity-temporal correlation (AE onset relative to ADA seroconversion); first-dose effects specific to biologics (cytokine release, infusion reactions); late-onset immune-mediated events (delayed autoimmune-like effects weeks-months after treatment); cumulative exposure effects; and step-up dosing safety patterns for bispecifics.
questions.rationale: Biologic safety profiles evolve differently over time than small molecules. CRS diminishes with repeated dosing. Immunogenicity-related AEs emerge as ADA develops (typically weeks to months). Immune-mediated AEs may have delayed onset. Understanding these biologic-specific temporal patterns is essential for accurate safety signal interpretation and risk management.

questions.question: Are emerging signals evaluated for biological plausibility, consistency across trials, and biologic-specific mechanism-based assessment (target biology, Fc effector function, immunogenicity mechanism)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether signal evaluation includes biologic-specific mechanistic assessment: target biology plausibility (on-target toxicity from target expression in normal tissues); Fc effector function assessment (ADCC/CDC-mediated cell depletion toxicity); immunogenicity mechanism (ADA-mediated immune complex disease, neutralizing antibody loss of efficacy); cross-reactivity assessment (binding to unintended targets); and class effect evaluation against other approved biologics with same target or mechanism.
questions.rationale: Biological plausibility assessment for mAb/bispecific safety signals requires mechanistic reasoning specific to biologic pharmacology. Understanding whether a signal is target-mediated, Fc-mediated, or immunogenicity-mediated determines the risk management strategy. Class effect evaluation against approved biologics provides regulatory context. This mechanistic assessment requires biologic-specific expertise.

questions.question: Is there an ongoing integrated benefit-risk assessment plan that accounts for biologic-specific risk dimensions (immunogenicity risk, infusion burden, immune-mediated AE risk, long-term immunological consequences)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether benefit-risk assessment incorporates biologic-specific dimensions: immunogenicity impact on efficacy (ADA-mediated loss of response) and safety (hypersensitivity, immune complex disease); infusion/injection burden on patient quality of life; immune-mediated AE risk and reversibility; long-term immunological consequences (immunosuppression-related infection and malignancy risk); CRS risk/benefit for bispecific T-cell engagers; and cross-reactivity risk from target expression in normal tissues.
questions.rationale: Biologic benefit-risk assessment includes dimensions absent from small molecule programs. Immunogenicity affects both benefit (loss of efficacy) and risk (hypersensitivity) simultaneously. Infusion burden affects patient quality of life. Long-term immunomodulation carries infection and malignancy risks. These biologic-specific dimensions must be explicitly incorporated into the benefit-risk framework, not treated as secondary considerations.

questions.question: Is benefit-risk documented for key milestones (FIH, end of Phase 2, pivotal trial entry, BLA/MAA)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether benefit-risk assessments are performed and documented at key development milestones, including BLA-specific benefit-risk review.
questions.rationale: Milestone-based benefit-risk documentation is a regulatory expectation. This applies to biologic programs with the additional milestone of BLA/MAA-specific benefit-risk review.

questions.question: Are emerging safety data incorporated into dynamic benefit-risk models, including biologic-specific data streams (immunogenicity trends, exposure-safety relationships, cumulative immune-mediated AE incidence)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether dynamic benefit-risk updating incorporates biologic-specific data: evolving immunogenicity incidence and clinical impact; exposure-safety relationships as PK data accumulates; cumulative immune-mediated AE incidence over longer treatment duration; CRS incidence and severity trends across dosing cycles; and infection rates for immunosuppressive biologics with longer follow-up.
questions.rationale: Biologic safety profiles evolve differently over time than small molecules. Immunogenicity data accumulates gradually. Immune-mediated AEs may emerge with prolonged exposure. CRS patterns change across dosing cycles. Dynamic benefit-risk models must incorporate these evolving biologic-specific data streams to remain current and decision-relevant.

questions.question: Are thresholds for tolerability and risk management clearly defined, including biologic-specific tolerability thresholds (maximum acceptable ADA incidence, CRS grade thresholds, infusion reaction rate limits)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether tolerability thresholds include biologic-specific criteria: maximum acceptable ADA incidence impacting efficacy; CRS severity thresholds triggering protocol modification (e.g., mandatory hospitalization above Grade 2); infusion reaction rate thresholds triggering premedication changes or rate modification; immune-mediated AE incidence limits; opportunistic infection rate limits for immunosuppressive biologics; and defined thresholds for benefit-risk reassessment.
questions.rationale: Biologic programs need defined tolerability thresholds for biologic-specific safety events. Without predefined thresholds, decisions about CRS management intensification, premedication changes, or protocol modification become ad hoc and inconsistent. Regulatory agencies expect sponsors to define and justify safety thresholds for known biologic-specific risks.

questions.question: Are protocols designed with appropriate eligibility criteria and risk mitigation strategies, including biologic-specific risk mitigations (premedication, step-up dosing, observation periods, CRS management algorithms)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether protocol risk mitigations include biologic-specific measures: premedication protocols for infusion reactions (corticosteroids, antihistamines, antipyretics); step-up dosing schedules for CRS-risk bispecifics; post-infusion/injection observation periods (duration, monitoring requirements); CRS management algorithms with tocilizumab or equivalent access; exclusion of patients with prior severe allergic reactions to biologics; body weight limits for weight-based dosing; and concomitant immunosuppressant restrictions or requirements.
questions.rationale: Biologic protocol risk mitigations are fundamentally different from small molecules. Step-up dosing, premedication, observation periods, and CRS management algorithms have no small molecule parallel. These biologic-specific risk mitigations must be evidence-based, clearly specified, and operationally feasible at sites. Inadequate risk mitigation in biologic protocols is a patient safety risk and a regulatory concern.

questions.question: Are stopping rules, dose modification criteria, and safety monitoring well justified, with biologic-specific dose modification guidance (ADA-driven modifications, CRS-driven step-down, infusion reaction rate modifications)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether dose modification criteria address biologic-specific scenarios: dose reduction or interruption for CRS; infusion rate modification for infusion reactions; dose adjustment or discontinuation for immunogenicity-driven loss of efficacy; dose modification for immune-mediated adverse events; re-challenge criteria after biologic-specific AEs; and step-up dosing re-initiation rules after treatment interruption.
questions.rationale: Biologic dose modification is more complex than small molecule dose adjustment. CRS may require temporary dose reduction with re-escalation. Infusion reactions may be managed with rate modification rather than dose change. Immunogenicity may require dose intensification (to overcome ADA) or discontinuation. Re-challenge after biologic-specific AEs has unique safety considerations (re-immunization risk). These scenarios must be explicitly addressed in protocol stopping rules.

questions.question: Are risks anticipated from nonclinical or earlier phase data appropriately addressed, including biologic-specific nonclinical findings (tissue cross-reactivity, exaggerated pharmacology, species-specific immunogenicity, CRS in NHP)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether nonclinical-to-clinical risk translation addresses biologic-specific findings: tissue cross-reactivity (TCR) binding in unexpected tissues and clinical monitoring implications; exaggerated pharmacology signals from target biology; species-specific immunogenicity findings and translation to human risk; CRS or cytokine elevation in NHP studies and implications for FIH design; Fc-mediated effector function findings and clinical safety monitoring; and reproductive toxicity findings relevant to FcRn-mediated placental transfer of IgG.
questions.rationale: Nonclinical-to-clinical safety translation for biologics requires different reasoning than small molecules. TCR findings identify potential on-target/off-tumour risks. Species-specific immunogenicity in animals may not predict human immunogenicity. CRS in NHP informs FIH step-up dosing design. IgG placental transfer via FcRn informs reproductive risk assessment. These biologic-specific nonclinical findings must be explicitly translated into clinical safety monitoring plans.

questions.question: Are trial participants stratified or excluded based on safety biomarkers or comorbidities, including biologic-specific exclusion criteria (prior biologic hypersensitivity, hepatitis B/C status for immunosuppressive biologics, pre-existing autoimmune conditions)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether eligibility criteria address biologic-specific safety concerns: prior severe allergic/anaphylactic reaction to any biologic product; hepatitis B/C and HIV screening for immunosuppressive biologics; pre-existing autoimmune conditions for immunomodulatory biologics; latent tuberculosis screening for immunosuppressive biologics; vaccination status and live vaccine restrictions; and prior biologic therapy washout requirements (to avoid overlapping immunosuppression or immunogenicity carryover).
questions.rationale: Biologic eligibility criteria include safety-driven exclusions that do not apply to small molecule trials. Hepatitis B reactivation risk with immunosuppressive biologics is a known serious safety concern. Pre-existing autoimmune conditions may be exacerbated by immunomodulatory biologics. Prior biologic hypersensitivity increases anaphylaxis risk. These biologic-specific exclusion criteria protect patients from known biologic class risks.

questions.question: Are safety data integrated across studies and presented in standardized formats (ISS, DSUR), with biologic-specific integrated analyses (Integrated Summary of Immunogenicity linked to safety, pooled infusion reaction analysis, cumulative immune-mediated AE summary)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether integrated safety analyses include biologic-specific pooled assessments: Integrated Summary of Immunogenicity (ISI) linked to safety outcomes; pooled infusion/injection reaction analysis across the development program; cumulative immune-mediated AE summary by organ system; exposure-safety analysis across dose levels and treatment durations; ADA-stratified safety analysis (ADA+ vs ADA− comparison); and integrated CRS analysis for bispecific programs.
questions.rationale: Biologic BLA/MAA submissions require integrated safety analyses that go beyond standard ISS. The ISI must be linked to clinical safety outcomes. Pooled infusion reaction and immune-mediated AE analyses are expected. ADA-stratified safety summaries are a regulatory requirement. These biologic-specific integrated analyses must be planned from program inception, not assembled retrospectively during filing preparation.

questions.question: Are cumulative safety data reviewed periodically by the sponsor's medical safety team?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether periodic safety data review is conducted with appropriate frequency and documentation.
questions.rationale: Periodic cumulative safety review is a fundamental pharmacovigilance requirement. This applies equally to biologic and small molecule programs.

questions.question: Are data visualizations used to support safety interpretation, including biologic-specific visualizations (ADA-safety correlation plots, infusion reaction time-course charts, CRS incidence by dosing cycle, immune-mediated AE onset timelines)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether safety visualizations include biologic-specific displays: ADA status overlaid on safety event timelines; infusion reaction onset time distribution relative to infusion start; CRS incidence and severity by dosing cycle (showing diminishing pattern); immune-mediated AE onset time distribution post-treatment initiation; exposure-safety scatter plots; and injection-site reaction severity trends over repeated dosing.
questions.rationale: Biologic-specific safety visualizations enable pattern recognition that is invisible in tabular data. CRS diminishing across cycles, infusion reaction clustering at specific time points post-infusion, and ADA-safety temporal correlations require visual display for effective interpretation. Standard safety visualizations designed for small molecules miss these biologic-specific patterns.

questions.question: Is there a formal process for adjudication of complex or unexpected events, including biologic-specific adjudication (infusion reaction vs CRS differentiation, immune-mediated AE confirmation, ADA-related event classification)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether adjudication processes address biologic-specific diagnostic challenges: differentiation between infusion reaction and CRS (overlapping symptoms, different mechanisms, different management); immune-mediated AE confirmation (distinguishing from background autoimmune disease); classification of events as ADA-related (requiring correlation with immunogenicity data); anaphylaxis confirmation using Brighton Collaboration criteria; and blinded endpoint adjudication for biologic-specific safety endpoints.
questions.rationale: Biologic safety events often require specialized adjudication. Infusion reactions and CRS can present with overlapping symptoms but require different management. Immune-mediated AEs must be distinguished from background disease. ADA-related events require correlation with immunogenicity data that may not be available at the time of the event. Formal adjudication processes prevent misclassification of these biologic-specific events.

questions.question: Are abnormal lab findings evaluated in context of expected biologic effects, including biologic-specific expected lab changes (lymphocyte depletion for anti-CD20, complement consumption, cytokine elevation, immunoglobulin reduction)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether laboratory interpretation accounts for expected biologic pharmacodynamic effects: lymphocyte/B-cell depletion for depleting antibodies; complement consumption for complement-fixing antibodies; cytokine elevation during CRS; immunoglobulin reduction with B-cell-depleting biologics; target cell population changes reflecting pharmacodynamic activity; and distinction between pharmacodynamic lab changes and toxicity signals.
questions.rationale: Biologic pharmacodynamic effects produce expected laboratory changes that must not be misinterpreted as toxicity. B-cell depletion from anti-CD20 is the intended effect, not a safety signal. Cytokine elevation during CRS is expected, not unexpected. Laboratory safety interpretation must distinguish between on-target pharmacodynamic changes and genuine toxicity signals — a distinction that does not arise in small molecule safety monitoring.

questions.question: Are predefined laboratory criteria used to detect toxicity signals, including biologic-specific laboratory monitoring (cytokine panels for CRS, complement levels, immunoglobulin monitoring, hepatitis B reactivation screening)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether laboratory monitoring includes biologic-specific panels: cytokine panel monitoring for CRS-risk biologics (IL-6, IFN-γ, CRP, ferritin); complement levels for complement-activating biologics; immunoglobulin quantification for B-cell-depleting biologics; hepatitis B viral load monitoring for immunosuppressive biologics; tuberculosis monitoring (QuantiFERON) for immunosuppressive biologics; and autoantibody panels for biologics with autoimmune-like AE risk.
questions.rationale: Biologic programs require laboratory monitoring panels that have no parallel in small molecule trials. CRS monitoring requires cytokine panels. Immunosuppressive biologics require viral reactivation screening. B-cell-depleting antibodies require immunoglobulin monitoring. These biologic-specific laboratory requirements must be pre-specified in the protocol and monitoring plan.

questions.question: Are central laboratories used with consistent normal ranges and alert flags?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether central laboratory services provide consistent reference ranges and automated alerting for critical values.
questions.rationale: Central laboratory use ensures data consistency. This applies equally to biologic and small molecule programs.

questions.question: Are vital signs and ECG abnormalities assessed systematically, including biologic-specific vital sign monitoring (pre/during/post-infusion vital signs, CRS-associated hemodynamic changes)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether vital sign monitoring includes biologic-specific requirements: pre-infusion baseline vital signs; intra-infusion vital sign monitoring at defined intervals; post-infusion vital signs during observation period; CRS-associated hemodynamic monitoring (hypotension, tachycardia, fever); and ECG monitoring if the biologic mechanism involves cardiac targets or if CRS-associated cardiac events are anticipated.
questions.rationale: Biologic infusion visits require vital sign monitoring at multiple time points (pre, during, post infusion) — a monitoring intensity not required for oral drug administration. CRS causes hemodynamic instability that requires systematic vital sign capture. These biologic-specific vital sign requirements must be standardized across sites.

questions.question: Are immunogenicity, hypersensitivity, and cytokine release being actively monitored with pre-defined criteria, grading scales, and management algorithms?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether biologic-specific safety topics are actively monitored: immunogenicity assessment per tiered testing strategy (screening, confirmatory, titer, NAb) with clinical correlation; hypersensitivity/anaphylaxis monitoring per Brighton Collaboration criteria; CRS monitoring and grading per Lee/ASTCT criteria with defined management algorithm; infusion reaction monitoring with standardized grading; and integration of immunogenicity data with clinical safety assessment.
questions.rationale: This question covers the three most important biologic-specific safety topics. For mAb/bispecific programs, immunogenicity monitoring, hypersensitivity/anaphylaxis surveillance, and CRS management are not optional — they are core safety requirements. Each requires pre-defined criteria, standardized grading, and documented management algorithms. This is the single most important safety question for any biologic program.

questions.question: Are QT/QTc and other cardiac safety signals evaluated, including biologic-specific cardiac considerations (CRS-associated cardiac events, troponin monitoring for T-cell engaging bispecifics, cardiotoxicity from target biology)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether cardiac safety assessment includes biologic-specific considerations: CRS-associated cardiac events (myocarditis, arrhythmia, heart failure in severe CRS); troponin monitoring for bispecific T-cell engagers; concentration-QTc analysis per ICH E14/S7B Q&A for biologics; target biology-mediated cardiac risk (if target is expressed in cardiac tissue per TCR); and cardiac monitoring requirements informed by nonclinical cardiovascular findings.
questions.rationale: While most mAbs have low intrinsic QTc risk, biologic-specific cardiac safety concerns exist: CRS can cause myocarditis and hemodynamic instability; T-cell engaging bispecifics carry troponin elevation risk; and some biologic targets are expressed in cardiac tissue. Cardiac safety monitoring must be tailored to the biologic mechanism rather than applying generic small molecule cardiac safety approaches.

questions.question: Are pregnancy exposures and outcomes tracked, with biologic-specific reproductive safety considerations (IgG placental transfer via FcRn, neonatal immune effects, live vaccine restrictions in exposed infants)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether reproductive safety monitoring addresses biologic-specific concerns: IgG placental transfer via FcRn (mAbs cross the placenta, particularly in third trimester); neonatal immune effects from in utero biologic exposure (B-cell depletion, immunosuppression); contraception requirements and washout periods reflecting biologic half-life; live vaccine restrictions in infants exposed in utero to immunosuppressive biologics; and pregnancy registry enrollment for exposed patients.
questions.rationale: mAbs and bispecifics cross the placenta via FcRn, exposing the fetus to pharmacologically active drug — particularly during the third trimester when FcRn-mediated transfer is most efficient. Neonates exposed in utero to immunosuppressive biologics may be immunocompromised and require delayed vaccination schedules. These biologic-specific reproductive safety considerations have no small molecule parallel.

questions.question: Is suicidality monitored for CNS or other high-risk programs (e.g., using C-SSRS)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether suicidality monitoring is implemented per FDA guidance for programs with CNS effects or neuropsychiatric risk.
questions.rationale: Suicidality monitoring per C-SSRS is required for relevant programs. This applies to biologic programs targeting CNS or with neuropsychiatric risk.

questions.question: Are investigators trained on AE/SAE reporting, safety endpoints, and biologic-specific risk mitigations (infusion reaction management, CRS recognition and grading, anaphylaxis protocols, immune-mediated AE workup)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether investigator training includes biologic-specific safety competencies: infusion reaction recognition, grading, and management; CRS recognition, grading per ASTCT/Lee criteria, and management (including tocilizumab administration); anaphylaxis recognition and emergency management; immune-mediated AE recognition and organ-specific workup; immunogenicity sample collection and ADA-related AE reporting; and step-up dosing safety monitoring for bispecific programs.
questions.rationale: Investigators must be trained on biologic-specific safety management that goes well beyond standard AE/SAE reporting. CRS grading and management, infusion reaction protocols, and immune-mediated AE workup require hands-on training and competency verification. Sites that cannot manage CRS or anaphylaxis should not enroll patients in biologic programs. Training must include competency assessment, not just attendance documentation.

questions.question: Are investigator brochures regularly updated with new safety findings, including biologic-specific safety sections (immunogenicity data, infusion reaction rates, immune-mediated AE profiles)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether IB updates include biologic-specific safety sections: updated immunogenicity data (ADA incidence, NAb rates, clinical impact); infusion/injection reaction rates and management updates; CRS incidence and severity updates; immune-mediated AE profiles and management recommendations; and cumulative safety data from the development program.
questions.rationale: Biologic IBs require dedicated sections for immunogenicity, infusion reactions, and immune-mediated AEs — safety categories that do not exist in small molecule IBs. Regular updates with evolving biologic-specific safety data are essential for investigator awareness and informed consent currency.

questions.question: Are sites informed rapidly about emerging safety issues or protocol amendments?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether safety communication to sites is timely, documented, and includes confirmation of receipt and understanding.
questions.rationale: Rapid safety communication is essential. This applies equally to biologic and small molecule programs.

questions.question: Are safety reports submitted on time (SUSARs, DSURs, IND annual reports), with biologic-specific safety reporting elements (immunogenicity data in DSURs, CRS summary in annual reports)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether periodic safety reports include biologic-specific content: DSUR sections on immunogenicity findings (ADA incidence, NAb rates, clinical impact); CRS summary in IND annual reports for bispecific programs; updated benefit-risk incorporating biologic-specific safety data; reference safety information (RSI) updates reflecting biologic-specific AESIs; and expedited reporting of biologic-specific SUSARs.
questions.rationale: DSURs and IND annual reports for biologic programs must include dedicated sections on immunogenicity, infusion reactions, and other biologic-specific safety topics. Regulators expect these biologic-specific elements. Omitting immunogenicity data from DSURs or CRS summaries from annual reports is a compliance gap that may trigger regulatory queries.

questions.question: Are all expedited and periodic reports consistent with regulatory expectations (FDA, EMA, PMDA)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates consistency and compliance of safety reporting across regulatory agencies.
questions.rationale: Multi-agency reporting consistency is a fundamental compliance requirement. This applies equally to biologic programs.

questions.question: Are reference safety information (RSI) and Investigator Brochure up to date, with biologic-specific RSI elements (immunogenicity-related events, infusion reactions, CRS, immune-mediated AEs)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether RSI includes biologic-specific expected events: immunogenicity-related clinical events (based on ADA data); infusion/injection reactions at expected frequencies; CRS at expected grade distribution; immune-mediated AEs by organ system; and mechanism-based expected toxicities from target biology.
questions.rationale: Biologic RSI must include biologic-specific expected events. If immunogenicity-related events, infusion reactions, or CRS are not listed in the RSI, they will be reported as SUSARs — creating unnecessary expedited reporting burden and potentially alarming investigators. Accurate biologic-specific RSI reduces noise and focuses attention on truly unexpected events.

questions.question: Are any clinical holds, IRB concerns, or safety-related protocol amendments documented?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether regulatory actions and safety-driven changes are tracked and resolved.
questions.rationale: Documentation of clinical holds and safety amendments is essential. This applies equally to biologic programs.

questions.question: Is the clinical trial safety data linked to the global safety database, with biologic-specific data integration (immunogenicity results linked to safety events, infusion data linked to reaction reports)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether data integration includes biologic-specific linkages: ADA/NAb results linked to AE reports in the safety database; infusion/injection administration data linked to reaction reports; PK exposure data linked to safety events for exposure-safety analysis; CRS grading data integrated into safety database; and immunogenicity-safety correlation data available for aggregate analysis.
questions.rationale: Biologic safety assessment requires integration of data streams that are separate in small molecule programs: immunogenicity data, PK data, infusion data, and clinical AE data must be linked for meaningful safety interpretation. Without these linkages, ADA-related safety signals are undetectable, exposure-safety relationships are invisible, and infusion reaction patterns cannot be correlated with drug administration data.

questions.question: Is reconciliation between clinical and safety databases performed and documented?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether database reconciliation is performed per schedule with documentation.
questions.rationale: Database reconciliation is a fundamental PV requirement. This applies equally to biologic programs.

questions.question: Are clinical trial and post-marketing safety managed in an integrated PV framework, with biologic-specific post-marketing safety considerations (long-term immunogenicity monitoring, biosimilar pharmacovigilance, interchangeability safety)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether PV framework integration addresses biologic-specific post-marketing requirements: long-term immunogenicity monitoring commitments; post-marketing immunogenicity studies (often FDA/EMA conditions of approval); biosimilar pharmacovigilance and interchangeability safety monitoring; biologic-specific REMS implementation; and product-specific vs class-wide signal detection for biologic products.
questions.rationale: Biologic post-marketing pharmacovigilance has unique requirements: immunogenicity monitoring often extends post-approval as a condition of licensure. Biosimilar switching/interchangeability raises safety questions requiring active surveillance. Product-specific vs class-wide signal detection is more complex for biologics than small molecules. The PV framework must be designed to accommodate these biologic-specific post-marketing obligations.

questions.question: Are safety findings communicated effectively, with biologic-specific safety communications (immunogenicity updates, infusion reaction rate changes, CRS management algorithm modifications)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether safety communications include biologic-specific content: immunogenicity data updates and clinical implications; infusion reaction rate changes and premedication modifications; CRS management algorithm updates; immune-mediated AE profile updates; and new biologic class-wide safety information relevant to the program.
questions.rationale: Biologic safety communications must include biologic-specific data that investigators need for patient management. Immunogenicity trends, infusion reaction pattern changes, and CRS management updates are critical for sites administering biologics. Generic safety communications that omit biologic-specific content leave investigators under-informed.

questions.question: Are newsletters, protocol updates, or safety bulletins issued as needed?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether safety information is disseminated through appropriate channels on a timely basis.
questions.rationale: Timely safety communication supports investigator awareness. This applies equally to biologic programs.

questions.question: Is there a safety escalation pathway for emerging risks, with biologic-specific escalation triggers (unexpected immunogenicity signal, CRS severity escalation, novel immune-mediated AE pattern)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether escalation pathways include biologic-specific triggers: unexpected immunogenicity incidence exceeding thresholds; CRS severity escalation or novel CRS pattern; new immune-mediated AE pattern not previously observed; infusion reaction rate exceeding expected frequency; anaphylaxis cluster; and immunogenicity-driven loss of efficacy affecting risk-benefit.
questions.rationale: Biologic safety escalation triggers differ from small molecule triggers. A spike in ADA incidence, an unexpected CRS severity pattern, or a novel immune-mediated AE require rapid escalation and expert assessment. These biologic-specific triggers must be pre-defined with clear escalation criteria, not left to ad hoc judgment.

questions.question: Are Phase 3 and long-term extension studies adequately powered to detect rare AEs, including biologic-specific rare events (progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy, serious opportunistic infections, malignancy with immunosuppressive biologics)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether the safety database size is adequate to detect biologic-specific rare events: PML with immunosuppressive/lymphocyte-depleting biologics; serious opportunistic infections; malignancy with long-term immunosuppression; rare immune-mediated organ toxicities; and delayed immunogenicity-related events.
questions.rationale: Biologic-specific rare events include some of the most serious safety concerns in the class: PML, opportunistic infections, and malignancy. These events require large safety databases or long follow-up for detection. Phase 3 and extension study design must be powered to provide adequate safety exposure for these biologic-specific concerns.

questions.question: Is there a Risk Management Plan (RMP) or REMS strategy developed, with biologic-specific risk minimization measures (infusion management requirements, CRS monitoring mandates, immunogenicity testing requirements, restricted distribution if needed)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether RMP/REMS addresses biologic-specific risk minimization: mandatory infusion protocols and observation periods; CRS monitoring and management requirements for bispecifics; immunogenicity testing at defined intervals; restricted distribution programs if safety profile warrants; prescriber and site qualification requirements for biologic administration; and patient education materials specific to biologic therapy risks.
questions.rationale: Biologic RMP/REMS programs include risk minimization measures that do not exist for small molecules: mandatory infusion protocols, CRS management requirements, prescriber qualification, and immunogenicity monitoring. These measures must be operationally feasible and scalable to commercial use. REMS design should begin during late-phase development, not post-approval.

questions.question: Are plans in place for post-marketing surveillance (PASS, registries), with biologic-specific post-marketing commitments (long-term immunogenicity registries, pregnancy exposure registries, malignancy surveillance for immunosuppressive biologics)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether post-marketing plans include biologic-specific surveillance: long-term immunogenicity monitoring studies; pregnancy exposure registries (given IgG placental transfer); malignancy surveillance registries for immunosuppressive biologics; infection surveillance for immunomodulatory biologics; and interchangeability/switching studies if applicable.
questions.rationale: Biologic post-marketing commitments typically include immunogenicity monitoring studies, pregnancy registries, and long-term safety surveillance that extend beyond standard small molecule PASS requirements. These commitments should be anticipated during development and resourced appropriately. Regulators increasingly impose these commitments as conditions of biologic approval.

questions.question: Is a named medical safety officer accountable for clinical safety strategy?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether a designated medical safety officer has clear accountability for the program safety strategy.
questions.rationale: Named safety accountability is essential. This applies equally to biologic programs.

questions.question: Is there an experienced cross-functional safety team with biologic-specific expertise (immunology, immunogenicity, biologic pharmacology, CRS management)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether the safety team includes biologic-specific expertise: immunologist or biologic pharmacology expert for mechanism-based safety assessment; immunogenicity specialist for ADA data interpretation and clinical correlation; CRS management expertise for bispecific programs; experience with biologic class safety profiles; and cross-functional integration with clinical pharmacology, bioanalytical, and immunogenicity teams.
questions.rationale: Biologic safety teams require expertise that is not needed for small molecule programs. Immunogenicity data interpretation, CRS management, and mechanism-based safety assessment require specialized biologic knowledge. A safety team without these competencies will misinterpret biologic-specific safety signals, miss immunogenicity-safety correlations, and provide inadequate CRS management guidance.

questions.question: Are external advisors or safety boards in place for high-risk programs, with biologic-specific advisory expertise (immunology KOLs, biologic safety experts, CRS management specialists)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether external safety advisory includes biologic-specific expertise: immunology KOLs for immune-mediated AE assessment; biologic safety experts with experience across the mAb/bispecific class; CRS management specialists for T-cell engaging bispecific programs; and regulatory safety experts with biologic BLA/MAA submission experience.
questions.rationale: External biologic safety expertise provides independent validation of safety strategy, signal assessment, and risk management for complex biologic programs. CRS management, immunogenicity safety assessment, and immune-mediated AE evaluation benefit from expert external input. This is especially critical for first-in-class biologics or bispecifics with novel mechanisms.

questions.question: Are tools in place for safety data mining and signal evaluation, configured for biologic-specific signal detection (immunogenicity-safety linkage, temporal pattern analysis, exposure-safety modeling)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether safety data mining tools are configured for biologic-specific analyses: immunogenicity-safety data linkage and correlation analysis; temporal pattern analysis relative to dosing cycles; exposure-safety modeling capability; CRS trend analysis tools; infusion reaction pattern detection; and immune-mediated AE clustering algorithms.
questions.rationale: Standard safety data mining tools designed for small molecule programs may not support biologic-specific analyses. Immunogenicity-safety linkage, cycle-dependent temporal analysis, and exposure-safety modeling require specific data integration and analytical capabilities. Safety tools must be configured to handle the multi-dimensional data relationships unique to biologic programs.

questions.question: Is a comprehensive immunogenicity safety monitoring plan integrated into the overall SMP, with defined criteria for clinical correlation of ADA with safety outcomes (hypersensitivity, loss of efficacy, immune complex disease, cross-reactive neutralization)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether immunogenicity safety monitoring includes: defined process for correlating ADA status with safety events in real-time; criteria for identifying ADA-related clinical events (hypersensitivity reactions in ADA+ patients, loss of efficacy temporally correlated with ADA seroconversion, serum sickness-like reactions, immune complex-mediated disease); cross-reactive neutralization risk assessment (ADA neutralizing endogenous protein counterpart); and escalation triggers when immunogenicity safety signals emerge.
questions.rationale: Immunogenicity safety monitoring bridges the bioanalytical immunogenicity program and clinical safety assessment. ADA data alone is insufficient — the clinical safety team must actively correlate immunogenicity results with clinical events to detect ADA-related safety signals. Cross-reactive neutralization (where ADA to a therapeutic protein neutralizes the endogenous counterpart) is a rare but potentially life-threatening risk unique to biologics. This integrated monitoring has no small molecule parallel.

questions.question: Is the clinical impact of immunogenicity prospectively assessed through pre-defined ADA-stratified safety analyses (ADA+ vs ADA− AE incidence, ADA-positive patient disposition, time-to-ADA and safety event correlation)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether the safety analysis plan includes pre-specified ADA-stratified comparisons: AE incidence and severity comparison between ADA+ and ADA− patients; SAE incidence by ADA status; hypersensitivity and injection-site reaction rates by ADA status and titer; PK exposure comparison in ADA+ vs ADA− patients (as context for safety differences); patient disposition analysis by ADA status (discontinuation rates, reasons); and time-to-event analysis correlating ADA seroconversion with safety endpoint onset.
questions.rationale: Pre-specified ADA-stratified safety analyses are a regulatory expectation for biologic BLA/MAA submissions. FDA and EMA review immunogenicity safety data by comparing outcomes in ADA-positive vs ADA-negative patients. These analyses must be pre-planned in the safety analysis plan — post hoc comparisons lack credibility. The analytical framework must account for ADA as a time-varying covariate.

questions.question: Is there a standardized infusion/injection reaction management algorithm in the protocol, with defined grading criteria, rate modification rules, premedication adjustments, re-challenge criteria, and permanent discontinuation thresholds?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether the infusion reaction management framework includes: standardized grading scale (NCI-CTCAE or protocol-specific); rate modification rules by grade (rate reduction, interruption, resumption criteria); premedication regimen and adjustments based on prior reaction history; re-challenge criteria after previous reactions (with graded re-introduction); permanent discontinuation criteria for severe or recurrent reactions; and post-infusion observation period requirements by reaction history.
questions.rationale: Infusion/injection reaction management is a core safety competency for mAb programs. Without standardized algorithms, sites manage reactions inconsistently — some discontinuing unnecessarily (losing patients), others continuing unsafely. A well-designed reaction management algorithm balances patient safety with treatment continuity. This algorithm should be evidence-based, operationally clear, and tested for clinical feasibility.

questions.question: For bispecific T-cell engagers, is a comprehensive CRS prevention, monitoring, grading, and management protocol in place, including step-up dosing rationale, tocilizumab availability, hospitalization criteria, and CRS-specific monitoring schedules?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether CRS management for bispecific programs includes: step-up dosing schedule with safety rationale (mitigating CRS by gradual dose escalation); CRS grading per ASTCT consensus criteria; CRS management algorithm with tocilizumab dosing, corticosteroid escalation, and ICU transfer criteria; tocilizumab availability confirmed at all sites before enrollment; hospitalization criteria for initial dosing cycles; CRS-specific monitoring schedule (vital signs, cytokine panels, organ function); and discharge criteria after CRS-risk treatment cycles.
questions.rationale: CRS management is the single most critical safety requirement for bispecific T-cell engaging antibodies. Inadequate CRS management can be fatal. The management protocol must be comprehensive, standardized across sites, and operationally validated. Tocilizumab must be physically available on-site before any patient receives a CRS-risk bispecific. This is a non-negotiable safety requirement.

questions.question: Is there a systematic surveillance program for immune-mediated adverse events, with organ-specific monitoring algorithms, diagnostic workup requirements, and management protocols for each target organ?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether immune-mediated AE surveillance includes: pre-defined organ-specific monitoring (hepatic, pulmonary, renal, endocrine, dermatologic, neurologic, cardiac, gastrointestinal, hematologic); standardized diagnostic workup algorithms for suspected immune-mediated events (biopsy criteria, autoantibody panels, imaging); management protocols for each organ system (immunosuppression escalation, specialist referral criteria, treatment interruption/discontinuation rules); and re-challenge criteria after immune-mediated AE resolution.
questions.rationale: Immune-mediated AEs are a defining safety challenge for immunomodulatory and immune-activating biologics. These events can affect any organ system, have variable onset timing, and require organ-specific diagnostic workup and management. A systematic surveillance program with pre-defined algorithms ensures consistent detection, diagnosis, and management across sites and investigators.

questions.question: Are immune-mediated AE patterns tracked across the program to identify organ-specific susceptibility profiles, risk factors, and optimal management strategies, feeding into benefit-risk assessment and labeling strategy?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether immune-mediated AE data is tracked longitudinally and analytically to inform: organ-specific incidence and severity profiles; time-to-onset distributions by organ system; risk factor identification (prior autoimmune history, concomitant medications, genetic factors); management outcome analysis (time to resolution, steroid requirements, recurrence rates); and translation of immune-mediated AE profiles into benefit-risk assessment and proposed labeling language.
questions.rationale: Immune-mediated AE patterns provide critical data for benefit-risk assessment, labeling, and risk management planning. Understanding which organ systems are most commonly affected, the typical time-to-onset, risk factors, and management outcomes enables evidence-based labeling and prescribing guidance. This analysis must be prospectively planned and integrated into the overall safety evaluation.

questions.question: Is there a defined process for distinguishing between pharmacodynamic effects (intended mechanism) and safety signals for immunomodulatory/immune-activating biologics, with documented criteria for when an expected pharmacodynamic effect crosses the threshold into a safety concern?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether the safety team has established: formal criteria for distinguishing expected pharmacodynamic effects from safety signals (e.g., at what degree does expected lymphocyte reduction become clinically concerning?); thresholds for escalation when pharmacodynamic effects exceed expected ranges; criteria for when cytokine elevation in CRS transitions from manageable to dangerous; and documented decision framework for immunosuppression-related infection risk vs intended immunomodulation.
questions.rationale: The most challenging safety question in biologic programs is distinguishing intended pharmacodynamic effects from genuine toxicity. B-cell depletion is the intended effect of anti-CD20 — but how much depletion becomes an unacceptable infection risk? CRS is an expected pharmacodynamic consequence of T-cell engagement — but when does it become life-threatening? This PD-vs-safety boundary must be formally defined, not left to individual clinician judgment.

questions.question: Is there a documented class-wide safety monitoring strategy that incorporates learnings from other approved biologics with the same target, mechanism, or structural class, and are competitive safety intelligence processes in place?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether the safety strategy includes: systematic review of safety profiles of approved biologics with the same target or mechanism; incorporation of class-wide safety signals (e.g., PML risk with anti-integrin, cardiac risk with certain mechanisms); monitoring for class-wide AESIs identified from post-marketing experience with related biologics; competitive safety intelligence process (monitoring new safety signals from competitor products); and proactive assessment of whether class-wide risks apply to the development candidate.
questions.rationale: Biologic safety assessment should not occur in isolation. Post-marketing safety experience with approved biologics targeting the same pathway provides critical context. Class-wide safety signals (PML with certain immunosuppressive biologics, cardiac events with specific mechanisms) must inform the monitoring strategy for new candidates. Competitive safety intelligence enables proactive risk management rather than reactive signal detection.

questions.question: For immunosuppressive/immunomodulatory biologics, is there a comprehensive infection surveillance program, including screening for latent infections (TB, hepatitis B/C), monitoring for opportunistic infections, and defined management algorithms for infection during immunosuppressive biologic therapy?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether infection surveillance includes: pre-treatment screening for latent TB (QuantiFERON or PPD), hepatitis B (HBsAg, anti-HBc, HBV DNA), hepatitis C, and HIV; periodic rescreening during treatment for viral reactivation risk; opportunistic infection monitoring protocol (PML symptoms, fungal infection screening); prophylaxis guidelines (PCP prophylaxis, antiviral prophylaxis based on risk assessment); infection management algorithms (treatment interruption criteria, antimicrobial treatment, re-challenge after infection resolution); and defined criteria for vaccination status assessment and live vaccine restrictions.
questions.rationale: Infection surveillance is a critical safety requirement for immunosuppressive/immunomodulatory biologics that is often inadequately structured. Hepatitis B reactivation has caused deaths in patients receiving immunosuppressive biologics. PML, though rare, requires active surveillance. Opportunistic infections may present atypically in immunosuppressed patients. A comprehensive, protocol-defined infection surveillance program is essential — ad hoc screening is insufficient.

questions.question: Is there an integrated safety-PK-immunogenicity analysis framework that enables real-time correlation of safety events with drug exposure levels and ADA status, supporting mechanism-based safety interpretation?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether an integrated analytical framework exists for: real-time linkage of safety events with drug concentration (exposure at time of AE); real-time correlation of safety events with ADA status and titer; mechanism-based safety interpretation using PK-PD-safety triangulation; population-level exposure-safety modeling; identification of exposure thresholds for safety events; and data infrastructure supporting cross-domain queries (clinical safety + PK + immunogenicity databases).
questions.rationale: The most sophisticated biologic safety assessment requires integration of three data streams: clinical safety events, PK exposure data, and immunogenicity results. An AE occurring at high drug exposure has different implications than the same AE occurring at low exposure due to ADA-mediated clearance. This safety-PK-immunogenicity triangulation enables mechanism-based safety interpretation that is impossible when these data streams remain siloed.

questions.question: Is the safety database adequacy assessment performed per ICH E1 guidelines, with biologic-specific considerations for safety database size (longer exposure durations needed for immunogenicity signal detection, delayed immune-mediated AE emergence, cumulative immunosuppression effects)?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether safety database adequacy assessment addresses biologic-specific considerations: extended exposure requirements for immunogenicity signal detection (ADA may take months to years to develop clinical consequences); longer follow-up needed for delayed immune-mediated AEs; cumulative exposure analysis for immunosuppression-related risks (infection, malignancy); and adequate representation of special populations (elderly, hepatic/renal impairment, pediatric if applicable) at the exposure durations relevant to biologic safety assessment.
questions.rationale: ICH E1 safety database requirements (1500 exposed, 300-600 for 6 months, 100 for 12 months) are minimum standards. Biologic programs may need larger databases or longer follow-up to detect immunogenicity-driven safety signals, delayed immune-mediated AEs, or cumulative immunosuppression effects. Safety database adequacy assessment must explicitly consider these biologic-specific exposure-time requirements, not merely demonstrate ICH E1 compliance.

questions.question: For FIH and early-phase biologic studies, is the starting dose safety assessment based on appropriate biologic-specific methodology (MABEL for immune-activating biologics, NOAEL with appropriate allometric scaling for non-immune-activating mAbs), with defined dose escalation safety rules?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether FIH dose selection and escalation safety assessment includes: MABEL-based starting dose for immune-activating biologics and bispecific T-cell engagers (per EMA guideline and FDA draft guidance); NOAEL-based starting dose with appropriate allometric scaling for non-immune-activating mAbs; sentinel dosing procedures for first patients at each dose level; cohort review and DSMB evaluation gates between dose levels; dose-limiting toxicity (DLT) definitions appropriate for biologic pharmacology (including CRS, infusion reactions); and defined escalation rules (modified Fibonacci, Bayesian, model-based) with safety stopping boundaries.
questions.rationale: FIH safety for biologics requires modality-specific dose selection methodology. MABEL is required for immune-activating biologics to prevent catastrophic first-dose events (TGN1412 precedent). Sentinel dosing, cohort review gates, and biologic-appropriate DLT definitions are essential safety measures. Standard small molecule dose escalation approaches are insufficient for biologic programs, particularly bispecific T-cell engagers.

questions.question: For bispecific T-cell engagers, is immune effector cell-associated neurotoxicity syndrome (ICANS) prospectively monitored with standardized grading (ASTCT/ICE score), defined management algorithms, seizure prophylaxis protocols, and differentiation criteria from CRS-associated neurological symptoms?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether ICANS surveillance includes: ASTCT-consensus ICANS grading using ICE (Immune Effector Cell-Associated Encephalopathy) score; defined monitoring schedule (neurological assessment frequency by treatment cycle); management algorithm with corticosteroid escalation, seizure prophylaxis, and ICU transfer criteria; differentiation criteria distinguishing ICANS from CRS-associated neurological symptoms, disease progression, and other causes of altered mental status; ICANS-specific data capture in EDC with standardized fields; and training for investigators on ICE scoring and ICANS recognition.
questions.rationale: ICANS co-occurs with CRS in bispecific T-cell engagers and can be life-threatening (seizures, cerebral edema, obtundation). FDA added a boxed warning for immune effector cell-associated enterocolitis to CARVYKTI in October 2025, demonstrating active regulatory focus on immune effector cell toxicities. ICANS requires distinct monitoring, grading, and management from CRS — treating it as a subset of CRS is clinically inadequate. Any bispecific TCE program without a dedicated ICANS protocol has a critical safety gap.

questions.question: Has the risk of ADA cross-reacting with and neutralizing the endogenous counterpart of the therapeutic protein been formally assessed, with a prospective monitoring plan if the risk is non-negligible?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether cross-reactive neutralization risk assessment includes: identification of whether an endogenous counterpart exists for the therapeutic protein target; in silico and in vitro assessment of sequence homology between therapeutic and endogenous protein at epitope level; prospective monitoring plan for clinical consequences of endogenous protein neutralization (e.g., monitoring endogenous protein levels, monitoring for deficiency syndrome); defined clinical criteria for identifying cross-reactive neutralization events; and rapid response protocol if cross-reactive neutralization is detected (treatment discontinuation, replacement therapy, patient follow-up).
questions.rationale: In November 2025, FDA investigated a death due to neutralizing antibodies to ADAMTS13 following Adzynma (recombinant ADAMTS13) treatment — ADA that neutralized both the therapeutic and the endogenous protein. This is the most dangerous immunogenicity outcome possible: the patient loses both the therapeutic and their own endogenous protein. For any biologic where an endogenous counterpart exists (erythropoietin, thrombopoietin, FVIII, ADAMTS13, etc.), this risk must be formally assessed and monitored. Even for mAbs where direct cross-reactivity is less likely, the assessment must be documented.

questions.question: Is there a defined process for assessing the clinical safety implications of manufacturing process changes (new cell line, facility transfer, scale-up, formulation change), including evaluation of altered immunogenicity risk, product quality attribute changes, and post-change safety monitoring requirements?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether the safety team participates in manufacturing change assessment: clinical safety input into comparability protocols (what safety endpoints need monitoring post-change?); evaluation of altered immunogenicity risk from process changes (aggregation, glycosylation, charge variants can affect immunogenicity); post-change safety monitoring plan (enhanced ADA sampling, targeted AE surveillance); review of product quality attribute differences and their potential safety implications; and integration of CMC comparability data with clinical safety data for regulatory submissions.
questions.rationale: Biologic manufacturing process changes can alter immunogenicity and safety profiles in ways that have no small molecule parallel. Minor changes in glycosylation can affect Fc effector function and safety. Increased aggregation can drive immunogenicity. 74% of CRLs issued 2020–2024 cited CMC deficiencies, and for biologics, CMC issues directly impact clinical safety. The safety team must be involved in manufacturing change assessment — this cannot be a CMC-only decision. Cross-functional safety-CMC governance for process changes is essential.

questions.question: Is a prospective safety labeling strategy being developed in parallel with safety data collection, including proposed Warnings & Precautions language, Adverse Reactions table design, boxed warning assessment, immunogenicity labeling per FDA draft guidance, and contraindication evaluation?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether safety labeling planning includes: prospective Warnings & Precautions drafting as safety data accumulates; Adverse Reactions table design (categorization, frequency bands, severity data presentation); formal boxed warning assessment (does any safety finding meet boxed warning criteria?); immunogenicity labeling per FDA 2022 draft guidance on immunogenicity information in labeling (ADA incidence, NAb rates, clinical impact statement, assay limitations); contraindication evaluation based on safety data; and proposed REMS language if applicable. Assesses whether labeling strategy is integrated into the overall regulatory filing plan.
questions.rationale: Safety labeling for biologics is more complex than small molecules. FDA's 2022 draft guidance on immunogenicity labeling specifies required content including ADA incidence, NAb rates, clinical impact assessment, and assay limitation disclosures. Biologic labels must also include infusion reaction warnings, CRS management (for bispecifics), and immune-mediated AE sections. Developing labeling language retrospectively during BLA preparation — rather than prospectively as data accumulates — results in rushed, inadequate labeling that triggers FDA review questions and delays approval.

questions.question: Is a structured, FDA/EMA-aligned benefit-risk framework being used (e.g., FDA's 5-dimension framework, PrOACT-URL, BRAT), with biologic-specific adaptations for immunogenicity impact, administration burden, immune-mediated AE risk, and long-term immunological consequences?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether benefit-risk assessment uses a recognized structured framework: FDA's benefit-risk framework (Analysis of Condition, Current Treatment Options, Benefit, Risk, Risk Management); or EMA-aligned PrOACT-URL or BRAT framework; with biologic-specific adaptations including: immunogenicity as a distinct risk dimension (impact on both benefit and risk); administration burden (infusion time, observation periods, injection training) as a patient-relevant dimension; immune-mediated AE risk as a separate safety dimension from acute toxicity; long-term immunological consequences (infection, malignancy) for immunosuppressive biologics; and documented quantitative or semi-quantitative scoring with sensitivity analyses.
questions.rationale: Regulators expect structured benefit-risk assessment — not narrative summaries. FDA's own framework has five defined dimensions. For biologics, the framework must be adapted to capture biologic-specific dimensions that do not exist in small molecule assessment: immunogenicity impacts both benefit (loss of efficacy) and risk (hypersensitivity); administration burden is a major patient-relevant factor; and long-term immunological consequences require extended time horizons. Ad hoc benefit-risk narratives are insufficient for Advisory Committee presentations and BLA submissions.

questions.question: Are pediatric-specific biologic safety considerations addressed, including differential immunogenicity profiles in children, vaccine schedule interactions with immunosuppressive biologics, growth and development effects from long-term immune modulation, and weight-based dosing safety across pediatric age groups?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether pediatric safety planning includes: anticipated differential immunogenicity in pediatric populations (children may have higher or lower ADA rates depending on immune maturity); vaccine schedule interaction assessment for immunosuppressive biologics (live vaccine contraindications, vaccine response monitoring); growth and development monitoring for biologics affecting immune, endocrine, or musculoskeletal systems; weight-based dosing safety across pediatric age/weight bands (neonates through adolescents); age-appropriate AE assessment tools (pediatric-specific PROs, developmental milestones); and extrapolation strategy safety considerations if pediatric efficacy is extrapolated from adult data.
questions.rationale: Pediatric studies are required per PREA (FDA) and PIP (EMA) for most biologic programs. Children may have different immunogenicity profiles than adults due to immune system maturity. Immunosuppressive biologics interact with vaccination schedules — live vaccines are contraindicated, and vaccine responses may be impaired. Long-term immune modulation during growth and development raises concerns absent in adult populations. These pediatric-specific biologic safety considerations must be addressed prospectively in the Pediatric Study Plan.

questions.question: For programs pursuing accelerated approval, is the safety monitoring framework adequate for the dual-phase period when commercial use and confirmatory trial safety evaluation occur simultaneously, with defined criteria for safety-triggered accelerated approval withdrawal?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether accelerated approval safety planning includes: enhanced post-marketing safety surveillance during confirmatory trial period; integration of commercial safety data with confirmatory trial safety data; defined criteria for when post-marketing safety signals would trigger accelerated approval withdrawal consideration; REMS requirements during accelerated approval period; and safety communication strategy if new safety signals emerge while product is commercially available. Addresses the unique regulatory context where patients are exposed commercially while safety evaluation is ongoing.
questions.rationale: Several biologic products have been withdrawn or had approval modified following accelerated approval — belantamab mafodotin was voluntarily withdrawn in 2023 after confirmatory trial failure, Elevidys received a boxed warning and indication restriction in November 2025 following liver failure deaths. The safety monitoring framework during accelerated approval must account for the commercial exposure occurring simultaneously with ongoing safety evaluation. This dual-phase safety monitoring has unique requirements including pharmacovigilance integration, real-world safety data collection, and defined withdrawal criteria.

questions.question: For biologic combination therapies (mAb + mAb, mAb + chemotherapy, bispecific + checkpoint inhibitor), is the overlapping and additive toxicity profile systematically assessed, with pre-defined monitoring for combination-specific risks (additive immunosuppression, overlapping immune-mediated AEs, altered immunogenicity from concomitant therapy)?
