questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether combination safety assessment includes: systematic evaluation of overlapping toxicity profiles between combination partners; additive immunosuppression risk assessment (cumulative infection risk, malignancy risk); overlapping immune-mediated AE risk (e.g., checkpoint inhibitor + bispecific both causing hepatitis); altered immunogenicity assessment (concomitant immunosuppressants may reduce ADA incidence but mask immunogenicity signal until treatment change); combination-specific dose modification rules (which agent to hold, reduce, or discontinue for specific AEs); and attribution algorithms for AEs occurring during combination therapy.
questions.rationale: Biologic combination therapies are now standard in oncology and increasingly common in autoimmune disease. Combination safety assessment is fundamentally more complex than monotherapy: overlapping immune-mediated AE profiles create additive risk, concurrent immunosuppression compounds infection risk, and AE attribution between combination partners is challenging. Concomitant immunosuppressants (e.g., methotrexate with anti-TNF) can mask immunogenicity during combination treatment, with ADA emerging when the immunosuppressant is stopped. These combination-specific safety challenges require systematic pre-planned assessment.

questions.question: For licensed-in, licensed-out, or co-developed biologic programs, is there a documented safety data transfer plan ensuring signal detection continuity, complete safety database migration, RSI alignment, and uninterrupted pharmacovigilance during transitions?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether safety transition planning includes: complete safety database migration with data integrity verification; signal detection continuity plan (no gap in aggregate safety review during transition); RSI and IB alignment between outgoing and incoming parties; SUSAR reporting responsibility clarity during transition; pharmacovigilance agreement defining ongoing safety data exchange obligations; and immunogenicity data transfer (ADA assay validation data, cut-point information, historical immunogenicity results) — a biologic-specific element that has no small molecule parallel.
questions.rationale: Biologic co-development and licensing transactions are common, and safety data transitions are high-risk events. Signal detection continuity gaps during transitions can allow safety signals to go undetected. Immunogenicity data transfer is uniquely complex for biologics — ADA assay platforms, cut-points, and historical results must transfer completely or immunogenicity safety assessment becomes impossible. Incomplete safety transitions have resulted in regulatory findings and delayed signal detection in real-world cases.

questions.question: Is post-marketing real-world safety evidence from approved biologics in the same class systematically reviewed and integrated into the development program's safety monitoring strategy, including registry data, FAERS/EudraVigilance mining, and published post-marketing safety studies?
questions.rubric_tests: Evaluates whether real-world evidence integration includes: systematic review of FAERS/EudraVigilance data for approved biologics with the same target or mechanism; review of post-marketing safety publications and registry data for class-relevant safety signals; incorporation of class-wide post-marketing findings into the development program's AESI list; evaluation of whether post-marketing safety signals from class peers are monitored prospectively in the development program; and periodic update of the class-wide safety landscape assessment throughout development.
questions.rationale: The biologic safety landscape evolves continuously as approved biologics accumulate real-world safety data. Post-marketing signals from approved biologics in the same class — infections with immunosuppressive biologics, cardiac events with certain mechanisms, novel immune-mediated AEs — provide critical early warning for development programs. FDA's 2025 safety communications (Adzynma death from neutralizing ADA, Elevidys liver failure, CARVYKTI enterocolitis boxed warning) demonstrate that new biologic safety signals continue to emerge post-approval. Development programs that ignore class-wide post-marketing evidence are operating with an incomplete safety picture.
